fuelImpact: 2026-04-05 Trump pushed Hormuz ultimatum to April 6 at 8 PM Eastern Time (April 7 10 AM AEST) following Iranian government request. Threatens massive attacks on Iranian energy sites if Hormuz not reopened. Iran rejected ultimatum as 'helpless and nervous'. Iran allowing Iraqi ships through Hormuz without restrictions. [CBS News] [HIGH]

2026-04-05 ~5,000 additional US Marines and sailors deploying to Middle East to support Iran war, per three US officials. 2,500 Marines from 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit already arrived. Total US regional presence ~50,000 as buildup continues ahead of potential ground operations. [Sino Defence Forum] [MEDIUM]

2026-04-05 US commandos conducted hot extraction of injured F-15E WSO deep inside Iran after heavy firefight. Black Hawk struck by Iranian fire but flew on with wounded crew. WSO used SERE training to evade capture. Trump: 'most daring operation in US history'. 2,500 Marines from 31st MEU arrived Middle East; ~5,000 more deploying. Total US regional presence ~50,000. [New York Times] [HIGH]

2026-04-05 Iranian drones struck Kuwait's Shuwaikh oil sector complex (housing KPC headquarters), two power/desalination plants, and Bahrain oil storage — significant damage, no casualties. Kuwait joins Saudi Arabia, UAE as regional states hit by Iran. Escalation of Iran's Gulf-state retaliation campaign. [Reuters] [HIGH]
2026-04-05 Fuel reservoir hit at Russia's Primorsk, NORSI refinery on fire after drone attacks [Channel NewsAsia (CNA)] [MEDIUM]
2026-04-05 Commandos probed deep into Iran to rescue downed airman: US media [Channel NewsAsia (CNA)] [MEDIUM]
foodImpact: 2026-04-05 Even if the Strait of Hormuz opened tomorrow, it could take months for fuel prices to come down [Sydney Morning Herald / The Age] [MEDIUM]
2026-04-03 Wake-up call: how Telstra’s ‘unreasonable’ price rises may cause customers to hang up [The Guardian Australia] [MEDIUM]

2026-04-03 Guardian visual guide: Hormuz blockade disrupting Gulf fertiliser/urea exports; buyers waiting it out; supply bad but not catastrophic yet — worst case if conflict extends [The Guardian] [MEDIUM]

2026-04-03 FAO: World food prices rise to 6-month high; if Iran war exceeds 40 days and input costs stay high, farmers may reduce fertiliser use, plant less, switch crops — threatening 2026/27 supply [Reuters] [HIGH]
watch: 2026-04-05 Approximately 90 Australian SAS troops deployed to Middle East — likely Al Minhad Air Base UAE. Government neither confirms nor denies. Deployment appears to have occurred almost immediately when Iran-US war began. [The Nightly] [MEDIUM]
2026-04-05 Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown [The Guardian Australia] [MEDIUM]
2026-04-05 Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages [The Guardian Australia] [MEDIUM]
2026-04-05 Asian leaders assure Australia normal supply of fuel will continue; fuel now sourced from US, Mexico as Albanese diversifies supply chains [The Guardian] [HIGH]

ships.name: Largo Eagle
ships.positionNote: Indian Ocean — rounding Cape of Good Hope

ships.name: Nord Ventura
ships.positionNote: Mid-Atlantic — heading for Cape of Good Hope

ships.name: BP Tanker 1
ships.positionNote: Off West Africa — heading for Cape of Good Hope

southKorea.note: Export cap in force since March 13

hormuz.note: CLOSED — IRGC blockade + kinetic attacks on vessels

babMandeb.note: THREATENED — Houthis active, ~30 tankers near Saudi Yanbu

suez.note: All traffic around Cape of Good Hope

household.note: Based on ABS 2023-24 Monthly Household Spending Indicator + 2022-23 HES. Crisis surcharge: 3-5% applied to transport, food, utilities, goods, health (not rent/mortgage). Includes direct fuel cost + indirect surcharge pass-through.

tiers.name: Pensioner / Concession
tiers.notes: Pension $1,116/mo. Debt: $4,200. Liquid savings $2,500 — constrained by age pension assets test; 5yr cost erosion has depleted buffer. Crisis surcharge +$325/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$1,058/mo. Crisis total: -$1,383/mo. Depletes W9.7 — savings exhausted by war start.
tiers.savingsSource: RBA 2024 Bulletin: many lower-income households hold liquid assets less than their fortnightly income. 65-74 median cash $15,829 (Westpac 2025) but pensioners face assets test constraints and 5yr cost-of-living erosion. Estimate: 2–3 weeks accessible cash buffer.

tiers.name: Low Income Worker
tiers.notes: Min wage $2,128/mo. Debt: $27,500. Liquid savings $1,200 — near-zero buffer after 5yrs rental crisis. Crisis surcharge +$430/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$1,239/mo. Depletes W4.2 — runs out mid-February 2026, BEFORE the Iran war even begins.
tiers.savingsSource: Savings.com.au 2025: 50.2% of renters have less than $5,000; 27.4% have less than $1,000. Westpac 2025 median 30-34 = $1,104. After 5yrs of rental increases (20-30%+ in major cities), near-zero buffer is the norm for min wage renters.

tiers.name: Average Worker
tiers.notes: Median income $5,500/mo after tax. Debt: ~$488k. Liquid savings $6,500 — 5yr squeeze: 4.35% rates since May 2022, 7.8% peak inflation, mortgage repayments up 10% of income (RBA 2025). Westpac median 35-44 = $811 cash; average skewed by offset accounts. Crisis surcharge +$700/mo. Depletes W7.5 — exhausted before Iran war begins.
tiers.savingsSource: Westpac 2025: median 35-44 liquid cash = $811. Mortgage holder average $38,692 (Money.com.au 2025) but this includes offset/redraw — not freely accessible. RBA: mortgagors drew down savings buffers 2022-2025 under 4.35% rate pressure. Accessible cash estimate: $5-8k.

tiers.name: Middle Income
tiers.notes: $8,200/mo after tax. Debt: ~$1.05M. Liquid savings $18,000 — down from pre-pandemic buffer; debt service $8,875/mo exceeded income since 2022 rate rises, accelerating savings drawdown. Crisis surcharge +$900/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$7,598/mo. Depletes W10 — just after war start.
tiers.savingsSource: ABS 2021-22 distribution; Money.com.au 2025 mortgage holder average $38,692. Adjusted down ~50% for 2022-2025 drawdown: high debt service at 4.35% on $1M+ mortgage consumed cash reserves. Dual income helped maintain some buffer vs lower brackets.

tiers.name: High Income
tiers.notes: $13,000/mo after tax. Debt: ~$1.18M. Liquid savings $42,000 — down from higher pre-2022 buffer; $10,553/mo debt service consumed cash during 2022-2025 high-rate period. Asset-rich but cash-constrained (wealth is in property equity, not liquid). Crisis surcharge +$1,200/mo. Depletes W22.7 — survives longer but does not make it to W26.
tiers.savingsSource: Money.com.au 2025: mortgage holders average $38,692. High income bracket adjusted upward but constrained by $10,553/mo debt service on investment property ($1.18M). Asset-rich, cash-constrained. Some recovery after 2025 RBA rate cuts. Estimate: $40-50k accessible cash.

tiers.name: Retiree (Super)
tiers.notes: Super drawdown $3,800/mo + partial pension $1,100/mo = $4,900. Owns home. Debt: $32k (residual mortgage $22k nearly paid off + credit card $7k + BNPL $3k) → debt service $450/mo. 15% of 65+ carry housing debt; 39% carry credit card balance (Morningstar/NAB/RBA 2024-25). Crisis surcharge +$520/mo. Pre-crisis deficit: -$330/mo (debt service). Crisis total: -$850/mo. Still most resilient demographic — survives W26 with $33,860.
tiers.savingsSource: Morningstar Nov 2025: 15% of 65+ carry housing debt (double 1990 rate). Jacaranda/NAB 2024: 9% have active mortgage; 39% of 65+ carry outstanding credit card balance (highest of any age group). RBA: avg credit card balance $3,300. HILDA: 55+ household debt quadrupled 20yrs to $242k. Revised from $0 to $32k: residual mortgage $22k + credit card $7k + BNPL/personal $3k.

government.note: Excise cut expires Jun 30. Additional fiscal costs accelerate as price-shock tiers rise. At Crisis (12%) price-shock tier: government faces $5.5bn/month additional intervention cost.

cpiAlertTiers.label: Alert
cpiAlertTiers.description: Gov monitoring, strategic reserve monitoring activates

cpiAlertTiers.label: Warning
cpiAlertTiers.description: Reserve release triggers, national cabinet convenes

cpiAlertTiers.label: Crisis
cpiAlertTiers.description: Widescale rationing, price controls, compulsory acquisition, irreversible economic damage

inflation.note: 4-tier Price Shock Alert Framework. Regional price increase (9.8%) already in Emergency tier. NT Regional (14.2%) already in Crisis. National metro price increase (7.2%) in Warning. Peak expected May 2026. Food/fertiliser lag effect means worst still ahead — peak June-July 2026.

weeklyItems.label: Tomatoes
weeklyItems.basis: War baseline: freight + farm inputs surge (pre-war → Apr 2026)

weeklyItems.label: Courier freight
weeklyItems.basis: War baseline: diesel surge → last-mile surcharge (pre-war → Apr 2026)

weeklyItems.label: Domestic airfares
weeklyItems.basis: War baseline: jet fuel + route repricing (pre-war → Apr 2026)

weeklyItems.label: Bread
weeklyItems.basis: War baseline: wheat + transport + packaging (pre-war → Apr 2026)

dbEvents.headline: Trump pushed Hormuz ultimatum to April 6 at 8 PM Eastern Time (April 7 10 AM AEST) following Iranian government request. Threatens massive attacks on Iranian energy sites if Hormuz not reopened. Iran rejected ultimatum as 'helpless and nervous'. Iran allowing Iraqi ships through Hormuz without restrictions.
dbEvents.label: GEO

dbEvents.headline: ~5,000 additional US Marines and sailors deploying to Middle East to support Iran war, per three US officials. 2,500 Marines from 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit already arrived. Total US regional presence ~50,000 as buildup continues ahead of potential ground operations.
dbEvents.label: GEO

dbEvents.headline: US commandos conducted hot extraction of injured F-15E WSO deep inside Iran after heavy firefight. Black Hawk struck by Iranian fire but flew on with wounded crew. WSO used SERE training to evade capture. Trump: 'most daring operation in US history'. 2,500 Marines from 31st MEU arrived Middle East; ~5,000 more deploying. Total US regional presence ~50,000.
dbEvents.label: GEO

dbEvents.headline: Approximately 90 Australian SAS troops deployed to Middle East — likely Al Minhad Air Base UAE. Government neither confirms nor denies. Deployment appears to have occurred almost immediately when Iran-US war began.
dbEvents.label: AUSTRALIA

dbEvents.headline: Iranian drones struck Kuwait's Shuwaikh oil sector complex (housing KPC headquarters), two power/desalination plants, and Bahrain oil storage — significant damage, no casualties. Kuwait joins Saudi Arabia, UAE as regional states hit by Iran. Escalation of Iran's Gulf-state retaliation campaign.

dbState.hormuz_status: CLOSED — IRGC blockade + kinetic attacks on vessels
dbState.bab_el_mandeb_status: THREATENED — Houthis active, ~30 tankers near Saudi Yanbu
dbState.supply_ceiling: mid-April 2026 — 55 cargoes arriving/en route but Easter demand surge + servo outage deterioration signal supply will be exhausted within 2-3 weeks regardless

lastReport.summary: 5 new events: WSO rescue (US ground raid Iran), 5,000 Marines deploying + 31st MEU arrived (US force ~50,000), ~90 Australian SAS deployed to UAE, Kuwait Shuwaikh oil complex hit by Iranian drones (3rd Gulf state attacked), Trump deadline pushed to Apr 7 10AM AEST

feed.title: FRAN Review — Feed
feed.summary: Event flow is accelerating around fuel, refinery disruption, and Australian contingency measures. Priority is to separate transient headlines from structural deterioration.
bullets: Fuel and shipping incidents remain the dominant source of new risk signals.
Australian headlines increasingly shift from monitoring to contingency actions.
Watch for repetition versus genuinely new deterioration in refinery throughput, outages, and cargo timing.
feed.mostLikelyCourse: Continued dense event flow centred on fuel, shipping, and contingency policy actions, with signal overload becoming the main analytical challenge.
feed.mostDangerousCourse: A step-change from headline noise to confirmed systemic disruption in cargo timing, refinery output, or domestic rationing signals without enough warning time.

supply.title: FRAN Review — Fuel Levels & Supply
supply.summary: Australia is still being supported by rerouted cargoes and reserve releases, but refinery weakness and route disruption keep the system tight.
bullets: Fuel reserve depth is the key buffer, but not a long-run solution if refinery runs stay constrained.
Fleet arrivals buy time, yet route disruption raises replacement cost and timing risk.
Servo outages are the consumer-facing signal that wholesale stress is leaking into retail availability.
supply.mostLikelyCourse: Supply remains tight but functioning, with rerouted cargoes and reserves preventing immediate collapse while outages and delays stay elevated.
supply.mostDangerousCourse: Replacement cargoes slip, Asian refinery output falls further, and servo outages broaden into sustained regional shortages before new imports land.

markets.title: FRAN Review — Markets
markets.summary: Markets are now showing a two-layer shock: broad price increase acceleration plus sharp item-level inflation in fuel, fresh food, and transport-linked services.
bullets: price increase projections imply the inflation wave is not finished even if spot panic eases.
Item inflation since Jan 1 is strongest where fuel, freight, or perishability matter most.
Household stress should be read as the dollar translation of these market moves, not a separate contradiction.
markets.mostLikelyCourse: price increase continues climbing into May as fuel, freight, and perishables keep feeding through to retail prices even if the first panic spike eases.
markets.mostDangerousCourse: Inflation broadens from fuel and perishables into general goods and services, pushing regional price increase into crisis territory and locking in a longer second-round surge.

households.title: FRAN Review — Households
households.summary: The household model shows that debt amplifies the inflation shock. Low-liquidity households fail first, but leveraged middle cohorts are structurally exposed once debt service dominates cash flow.
bullets: Scenario A shows how inflation and debt compound into rapid savings depletion.
Scenario B isolates the pure cost-of-living shock and shows which groups remain vulnerable even without debt.
The key analytical question is not just who has low income, but who lacks enough buffer to absorb sustained item-price increases.
households.mostLikelyCourse: Low-buffer households continue depleting first, while highly leveraged middle cohorts deteriorate fastest once debt service and inflation compound together.
households.mostDangerousCourse: A prolonged inflation pulse plus delayed supply normalisation pushes even initially resilient households into rapid depletion, forcing distress selling or arrears.

domino.title: FRAN Review — Domino Cascade
domino.summary: The domino model translates the conflict into second- and third-order Australian effects over six months, linking military escalation to fuel, food, travel, and productivity outcomes.
domino.mostLikelyCourse: The base-case path remains a prolonged standoff where fuel and logistics stay disrupted long enough to drive inflation and slower productivity, but stop short of full systemic breakdown.
domino.mostDangerousCourse: A shift to ground war or a harder Hormuz closure forces the escalation path, making fuel rationing, essential-travel limits, and structurally higher food prices much more likely.
bullets: Domino scenarios are useful because they connect tactical events to civilian economic outcomes.
The biggest swing factor is whether shipping disruption remains partial or becomes a sustained closure problem.
Watch trigger events closely: ground-force entry, refinery attrition, and transport rationing signals.

troops.title: FRAN Review — Troop Deployments
troops.summary: The troop panel tracks whether the conflict is moving from coercive signalling into a posture consistent with sustained offensive operations or direct intervention.
troops.mostLikelyCourse: Force movements remain in a high-readiness deterrence posture, increasing pressure and signalling risk without immediately crossing into full ground-war deployment.
troops.mostDangerousCourse: A rapid transition from pre-deployment to committed operational staging shortens warning time and strongly increases the probability of a wider regional war.
bullets: Troop posture matters because it can convert market stress into a full escalation pathway very quickly.
The key issue is not only force size, but whether logistics, basing, and air/sea cover indicate intent to sustain operations.
Watch for changes in phase, transport tempo, and forward positioning around Gulf and regional bases.

troopDeployments.phaseReason: US has 50,000+ troops in Middle East (+10,000 above normal). Ground troops have arrived. Spec ops and limited ground ops confirmed. Full invasion pending political decision. UK/Australia explicitly not committing ground forces. 35-nation coalition is naval blockade, not ground force.
troopDeployments.invasionIndicatorReason: US spec ops inside Iran (confirmed). IDF spec ops inside Iran (confirmed). 50,000+ US troops in region. But UK/Australia explicitly refusing ground invasion. Major conventional invasion: not yet ordered. Decision pending Trump.
troopDeployments.supplyChainImplication: Ground invasion would extend war months = Hormuz/Bab el-Mandeb contested long-term = Australian fuel supply ceiling extends to Q3. Current buildup alone suggests sustained disruption regardless of invasion outcome.

invasionTimeline.description: Pre-deployment. US has real-time option for ground raids (spec ops on ground). Major allies UK/Australia refusing ground role. IDF spec ops inside Iran (confirmed). Full conventional invasion: not yet ordered.
invasionTimeline.earliestGroundOps: Now — spec ops are already operating inside Iran (Al Arabiya/Wiki). Conventional ground invasion: decision pending.
invasionTimeline.scenario: Two-track: US spec ops raids (active now) vs. full conventional invasion (not ordered). UK/Australia explicitly defensive-only. IDF limited ground ops confirmed.

us.name: United States

deployed.unit: USS Abraham Lincoln Carrier Strike Group

us.note: NYT (29 Mar): US troop levels in Middle East now exceed 50,000 — 10,000 above pre-war baseline. Ground troops HAVE arrived per Military.com (30 Mar). Spec ops operating. Decision on expanded ground ops pending Trump.

uk.name: United Kingdom

deployed.location: Reinforcing UK bases and Gulf allies

planned.unit: UK ground forces to Iran invasion
planned.status: REFUSED — UK NOT joining invasion. Trump publicly criticized UK for this.

uk.note: UK is naval/logistics only. Defence Secretary Healey confirmed ~1,000 UK troops in region but explicitly NOT committing to ground invasion. Trump jibed UK for refusing to 'get involved in the decapitation of Iran.'

australia.name: Australia

deployed.unit: ADF — 85 personnel + Boeing E-7 Wedgetail + AMRAAM missiles

planned.status: RULED OUT — Albanese: purely defensive action only

australia.note: Albanese explicitly ruled out combat ground troops. ADF SAS confirmed at Al Minhad (UAE). E-7 Wedgetail airborne early warning deployed to same area. HMAS Sydney also deployed to Gulf region.

israel.name: Israel

planned.unit: IDF full ground invasion of Iran
planned.status: AUTHORISED — awaiting execution order from Netanyahu

israel.note: IDF spec ops confirmed inside Iran (Al Arabiya, 29 Mar). Large-scale invasion authorised but not yet executed. Iran's parliament speaker said Iranian forces are 'waiting' for a US invasion. IDF prepared for multi-front war per CNN (28 Mar).

saudi.name: Saudi Arabia
saudi.note: Saudi Arabia providing basing and defensive support. No confirmed offensive ground deployment to Iran. Yanbu is a key staging area for coalition naval operations.

uae.name: UAE

deployed.unit: Australian ADF E-7 Wedgetail + missiles deployed here

uae.note: UAE hosting Australian/coalition forces. UAE itself is in defensive posture. Ruwais refinery hit by Iran (2 Apr) — UAE is a target, not an aggressor.

other.name: Other Coalition (34 nations)

deployed.location: Gulf of Oman / Hormuz approaches / Arabian Sea

other.note: 35-nation coalition is primarily a NAVAL escort/blockade force. Ground contributions from non-US nations are minimal to none. France is the most significant additional European deployer but focused on naval power projection.

equations: Scenario severity λ: resolution = 0, base = 1/3, escalation = 1

scenarios.name: Base Case — Stalemate
scenarios.description: Neither side achieves decisive advantage. US/Iran settle into attritional standoff without ground invasion. Impact remains channeled through fuel prices and shipping disruption only.

mobilityOutlook.summary: Mobility shifts into managed constraint: freight first, patchy regional fuel access, reduced discretionary travel, and persistent aviation disruption.
consequences: Freight and essential services are prioritised before private mobility.
Regional shortages stay uneven even if metro supply holds.
Domestic aviation remains reduced and expensive for longer.
Households feel availability and price pain before formal controls.

scenarios.name: Escalation — Ground War
scenarios.description: US deploys ground forces. Iran closes Hormuz. Full fuel shock, travel restrictions cascade into productivity and food. Food prices peak at +40% with permanent structural change.

mobilityOutlook.summary: Mobility risk becomes systemic. Freight gets protected, aviation shrinks, regional access degrades, and rationing becomes a live policy option.
consequences: Freight corridors, agriculture, emergency services and buses get priority access.
Domestic aviation and airfreight contract quickly if jet fuel tightens.
Regional household fuel access becomes patchy before metro rationing.
Formal allocation and movement controls become plausible if shortages deepen.

scenarios.name: Resolution — Ceasefire
scenarios.description: Trump-Khamenei back-channel holds. Temporary ceasefire agreed. Fuel prices ease within weeks. Food impact stays below +10%.

mobilityOutlook.summary: Mobility remains stressed but functional. Expect delays, higher prices, and selective conservation rather than formal restrictions.
consequences: Domestic flights remain expensive and patchy before recovering.
Voluntary fuel conservation is more likely than rationing.
Regional freight delays ease first if routes reopen.

keyDecisionPoints.label: Trump decision
keyDecisionPoints.description: US ground invasion vote — most critical fork

keyDecisionPoints.label: Rationing trigger
keyDecisionPoints.description: Diesel reserves <10 days — travel restrictions activate

keyDecisionPoints.label: Essential travel
keyDecisionPoints.description: Diesel reserves <7 days — non-essential movement prohibited

keyDecisionPoints.label: Ceasefire window
keyDecisionPoints.description: Historical pattern suggests back-channel talks begin Month 3 if stalemate holds

keyDecisionPoints.label: Supply reset
keyDecisionPoints.description: New normal established regardless of scenario

sentiment.description: 5-area sentiment index scored from event headlines. Scale: -10 (extreme stress) to +10 (full recovery). 3-day smoothed daily average.
